title: podman volume create --ignore chowns the volume back to root when the volume already exists
body:
### Issue Description

When mounting a volume in an image that uses a `User` layer, like TimescaleDB does [here](https://hub.docker.com/layers/timescale/timescaledb-ha/pg13.11-ts2.11.0-all/images/sha256-37d8f6340e45c9ac18522a548b404148c1ed3bc11680012d1ba0d01d6ef0f6b1?context=explore), restarting the container breaks the volume's permissions.

The volume ends up owned by root:root after the container is shut off. When restarting the container, the volume remains owned by root:root as opposed to the user from the `User` layer.

This can be fixed by changing permissions of the volume on the host before restarting the container.


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. File: timescaledb.container
```
[Unit]
Description=Postgres/TimeScale DB

[Container]
ContainerName=timescaledb
Image=docker.io/timescale/timescaledb-ha:pg15.3-ts2.11.0-all-oss
Exec=postgres -c timescaledb.license='timescale'
Environment=POSTGRES_PASSWORD=password123
PublishPort=5432:5432

Volume=timescaledb.volume:/home/postgres/pgdata/data

[Service]
Restart=on-failure

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target default.target
``` 

2. File: timescaledb.volume
```
[Volume]
```

3. `sudo systemctl start timescaledb`
4. After DB is initalized, `sudo systemctl stop timescaledb`
5. `sudo systemctl start timescaledb` <-- This will fail because the systemd-timescaledb is now mounted as root and the container runs as user `postgres` (UID: 1000)

To "hand fix":

6. `chown -R 1000:1000 /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-timescaledb/`
7. `sudo systemctl start timescaledb` <-- This works until the next restart.


### Describe the results you received

The container started with the volume mounted and owed by root.

### Describe the results you expected

The container to start with the volume mounted and owned by the same container user that it was during the first lifetime.

### podman info output

```yaml
If you are unable to run podman info for any reason, please provide the podman version, operating system and its version and the architecture you are running.


Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.5.0
API Version:  4.5.0
Go Version:   go1.20.2
Built:        Fri Apr 14 15:42:22 2023
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

OS is Fedora 38.

Note: I can not easily upgrade to 4.5.1 right now, but based on the change log, I don't think this issue would be addressed.
```


### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body:
that happens because the `.volume` file creates the volume before the container uses it.

You may want to force the `User` for the volume something like:

```
[Volume]
User=1000
Group=1000
```

or not specify a quadlet `.volume` at all.

@giuseppe But it works for the first start of the container, it only fails to work after a restart. I can also change the permissions manually, then re-start the (now failed) container, and everything will work again … until the container is stopped or restarted next. I don't see this behavior outside Qualet.

I will try adding the User and Group options to the Volume, but that seems to be a different requirement than podman or kube.

Volumes are automatically chowned on first use based on the in container ID, this seems to be working in your case. However something is chowning it back to root and I would not expect podman doing that.

Please check what permissions the volume has after the first stop. Note you should not chown the podman volume info, only the `_data` subdirectory should be enough.

Hi @Luap99. Thanks for the insight. I did a bit more testing, and found that acutally it requires a restart of the machine for this bug to show up. See my shell session below:

```
[root@CT103 systemd]# nvim timescaledb.container

[root@CT103 systemd]# nvim timescaledb.volume

[root@CT103 systemd]# systemctl daemon-reload

[root@CT103 systemd]# podman volume ls

[root@CT103 systemd]# systemctl start timescaledb
Job for timescaledb.service failed because a timeout was exceeded.
See "systemctl status timescaledb.service" and "journalctl -xeu timescaledb.service" for details.
```

***Note: This fails because it takes too long to download the image. Podman tries auto-restarting, and eventually it comes up***

```

***I expect `abalmos:abalmos` here, and everything works.***

```
[root@CT103 systemd]# systemctl stop timescaledb
[root@CT103 systemd]# ls -la /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-timescaledb
total 12
drwx------  3 root    root    4096 Jun 19 22:22 .
drwx------  3 root    root    4096 Jun 19 22:22 ..
drwx------ 19 abalmos abalmos 4096 Jun 19 22:24 _data
```

***All is well at this point***

```

***All is well after the restart. This is where my inital bug report is wrong. However, after a machine restart things are wonky.***

```
[root@CT103 systemd]# reboot
```

***Machine reboots, and we ssh back in***

```
[root@CT103 abalmos]# systemctl status timescaledb
× timescaledb.service - Postgres/TimeScale DB
     Loaded: loaded (/etc/containers/systemd/timescaledb.container; generated)
    Drop-In: /usr/lib/systemd/system/service.d
             └─10-timeout-abort.conf
     Active: failed (Result: exit-code) since Mon 2023-06-19 22:26:09 UTC; 16s ago

    Process: 1275 ExecStopPost=/usr/bin/podman rm -f -i --cidfile=/run/timescaledb.cid (code=exited, status=0/SUCCESS)

[root@CT103 abalmos]# ls -la /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-timescaledb
total 12
drwx------  3 root root 4096 Jun 19 22:22 .
drwx------  3 root root 4096 Jun 19 22:22 ..
drwx------ 19 root root 4096 Jun 19 22:25 _data
```

***The database fails to start becuase of volume permission errors***

```
[root@CT103 abalmos]# ls -la /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-timescaledb
total 12
drwx------  3 root root 4096 Jun 19 22:22 .
drwx------  3 root root 4096 Jun 19 22:22 ..
drwx------ 19 root root 4096 Jun 19 22:25 _data
```

The `_data` folder is now owned by `root:root`. This can fixed by first chowning the volume to `abalmos:abalmos` and then everything will run until the next ***machine*** restart.


```
[root@CT103 abalmos]# chown -R abalmos:abalmos /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-timescaledb/_data/

[root@CT103 abalmos]# ls -la /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-timescaledb
total 12
drwx------  3 root    root    4096 Jun 19 22:22 .
drwx------  3 root    root    4096 Jun 19 22:22 ..
drwx------ 19 abalmos abalmos 4096 Jun 19 22:25 _data

[root@CT103 abalmos]# systemctl start timescaledb

Ok I think I see the problem in the code. Running `podman volume create --ignore <name>`  will chown it back to root even if it already exists. This command is run by the volume unit before the container unit. So if you manually start the volume unit you should be able to reproduce this easily.
@Luap99 Your right, simply `systemctl restart timescaledb-volume` is enough to reproduce this. Should I report this separately, or clean up the issue?
No new issue needed, we have the full context here.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I think I'm running into this issue as well, although initially I thought it was an issue with Fedora CoreOS and re-running ignition against a persisted second disk mounted to `/var`.

I also ran into this on Fedora CoreOS 38 using ignition defined quadlets. First start was always owned by root, if I manually removed the volume and restarted the quadlet the volume was re-created with correct owner.

I followed @giuseppe advice and simply added User and Group to the [Volume] because I knew which user my container was running as, and now it works as intended on first boot.
